title: Punch-needle mug rug set
subtitle: Four punch-needle drink coasters, each 12 x 12 cm, in simple geometric designs on weaver's cloth
excerpt: A set of four punch-needle drink coasters worked on weaver's cloth stretched in an embroidery hoop. Each coaster measures 12 x 12 cm with a 1 cm wool or cotton pile. The four designs are a square inside a square, a filled circle, horizontal stripes, and a cross. Edges are bound with rug binding tape.
sourceNotes: Observation-led. The punch-needle hoop method for small textile pieces is documented in modern punch-needle texts. The Oxford punch needle is the industry reference tool for consistent pile height. The geometric designs (square-in-square, circle, stripes, cross) are standard introductory motifs with no named author or design source.

glossaryTerms.definition: A hollow-barrelled needle with an open slot along one side. Yarn is threaded through the barrel and emerges at the tip. The needle is pushed through a woven backing fabric from the back of the work, forming a loop on the front face. Each push-and-pull cycle creates one loop of the pile. The pile height is set by the depth stop on the handle or by the length of the barrel below the grip.

A tightly woven, even-weave cotton or linen fabric used as a backing for punch-needle work. The regular grid of threads allows the punch needle to pass through cleanly without splitting the warp or weft, and the close weave grips each loop securely so the pile does not pull out. Weaver's cloth is distinct from monks cloth, which has a looser, hessian-like open weave better suited to larger rug-hook work.

content.text: These four coasters are worked with a
punch needle
on
weaver's cloth

stretched in an embroidery hoop. Each finished coaster measures 12 x 12 cm with a 1 cm looped pile in DK-weight wool or cotton yarn. The four designs use only straight lines and curves drawn directly onto the backing: a square inside a square, a filled circle, horizontal stripes, and a cross. The pile side becomes the top face; the smooth cloth back rests on the table surface. Each coaster takes roughly 20 to 30 minutes to work once the backing is prepared.
What you need

items.name: Weaver's cloth (tightly woven even-weave cotton)
items.qty: 50 x 50 cm minimum for the set

items.name: DK wool or cotton yarn, main colour
DK yarn, contrast colour (optional for geometric detail)
Punch needle, set to 1 cm pile height
Rug binding tape, 2 cm wide
items.qty: Approx 60 cm per coaster (240 cm for the set)

items.name: Blunt needle, size 20, for threading and finishing ends
PVA or latex fabric glue (optional, to secure the backing edge)

content.text: Method
Step 1: Prepare the backing and transfer the design

Cut four squares of weaver's cloth at 20 x 20 cm each. The finished coaster will be 12 x 12 cm; the extra fabric gives the hoop enough material to grip without distorting the design area. On each square, find the centre and mark a 12 x 12 cm square with the permanent marker. This outer line will be the cut line after the work is finished.

Draw the design inside the 12 x 12 cm square on the back of the cloth (the side you will work from). For the square-in-square coaster, draw a second square 3 cm inside the outer line, so both squares share the same centre. For the circle coaster, find the centre of the 12 x 12 area, mark it, and draw a circle with a 4 cm radius (8 cm diameter). For the stripes coaster, draw horizontal lines at 2 cm intervals across the full width. For the cross coaster, draw two lines crossing at the centre: one horizontal and one vertical, each 2 cm wide (mark 1 cm either side of the centre on both axes).
Step 2: Stretch the backing in the hoop

Loosen the hoop fully, place the inner ring flat on a table, lay the weaver's cloth over it with the marked design facing down (the back of the work faces upward in the hoop, because punch-needle work is done from the back), and press the outer ring down over the cloth. Tighten the screw until the cloth is drum-taut. The threads of the cloth should run horizontally and vertically without distortion; if they pull on the diagonal, remove the cloth, realign, and re-mount. A slack or skewed backing causes uneven pile and loops that pull out.
Step 3: Thread the punch needle

Set the punch needle to 1 cm pile height by adjusting the depth stop on the handle or measuring from the tip. Cut a length of yarn about 1.5 m long. Feed the yarn through the hollow barrel from the handle end using the wire threading tool that comes with most punch needles, or use a folded length of fine wire as a threading loop. The yarn should emerge from the open slot at the tip of the needle. Pull 5 to 8 cm of yarn through the tip before starting to work, so the first loop has enough tail to hold.
Step 4: Work the pile from the back

Hold the hoop so the marked back of the cloth faces you. Push the needle tip straight down through the cloth at the starting point of the design until the barrel meets the cloth surface (the depth stop should prevent over-penetration). Pull the needle straight back up without pulling it fully out of the weave: the tip should just clear the surface. Move the needle one to two cloth threads across and push down again. Repeat. Each push forms one loop on the front face. The yarn feeds through automatically as you work; do not pull it from the back.

Work row by row inside the design lines. Start at the outer edge of each shape and work inward so the rows sit neatly next to each other. Keep the needle perpendicular to the cloth on every push; angling the needle distorts the loop height and can split adjacent loops. The open slot of the needle barrel should always face the direction of travel so the yarn feeds smoothly. Flip the hoop over regularly to check the pile is even and that no loops have been pulled back through inadvertently.

To change colour, cut the yarn at the back leaving a 3 cm tail and thread in the new colour. When restarting after a cut end, hold the tail on the back with a finger for the first two or three loops to stop it pulling through. Fill all four design areas completely before moving to the binding step.
Step 5: Secure the backing and cut out

Remove the worked cloth from the hoop and lay it pile-face down on a flat surface. Run a thin line of PVA or latex fabric glue along the back of the work just inside the 12 x 12 cm cut line, covering about 1 cm of the backing. Allow to dry fully (20 to 30 minutes). The glue locks the outer rows of loops so they cannot pull out when the edges are cut and bound.

Cut out each coaster on the 12 x 12 cm outer line, leaving a clean square edge. If the cloth frays, run a second line of glue just inside the cut edge immediately after cutting and allow it to dry before binding.
Step 6: Bind the edges

Cut a length of rug binding tape at 54 cm per coaster (48 cm for the perimeter plus 6 cm to overlap and join at the starting corner). Fold the tape in half lengthwise so it wraps around the cloth edge with half on each face. Starting at a corner, slip the tape over one edge of the coaster so the fold sits on the cut edge. Pin in place. Continue around all four sides, mitring the tape at each corner by folding a small diagonal pleat on the back face. Sew the tape through all layers with a blunt needle and matching thread, using a running stitch or a back stitch close to the inner edge of the tape. Overlap and stitch the join at the starting corner.
Common mistakes

items.problem: Loops pull out of the backing when the work is handled or when the hoop is removed
items.cause: The weaver's cloth was not taut enough in the hoop, or the needle was lifted at an angle rather than pulled straight back
items.fix: Re-stretch the cloth until drum-taut before working. Pull the needle straight back, not at an angle, so the loop lands flat against the back face. If loops are pulling out from a finished section, re-work those holes and apply a thin line of fabric glue across the back of the loose area before cutting out.

items.problem: The pile height is uneven across the coaster
items.cause: The depth stop shifted during work, or the needle was not pushed all the way to the stop on every stitch
items.fix: Check the depth stop is locked before each session by pressing the needle against a hard surface and confirming it meets the same resistance every time. Develop a rhythm: push until the stop touches the cloth, then lift. If some loops are noticeably longer, trim them flush with sharp scissors after the coaster is finished.

items.problem: The cloth backing puckers or the design area distorts
items.cause: The cloth was mounted with the grain on the diagonal, or the hoop was not tightened fully after re-mounting
items.fix: Align the cloth threads horizontally and vertically in the hoop before tightening. If puckering has already occurred in a finished coaster, dampen the back lightly, lay it pile-face up on a flat surface under a weight, and allow to dry flat.

content.text: Finishing and care

The rug binding tape edge is both decorative and functional: it protects the cut cloth from fraying and gives the coaster a finished appearance from the side. If the tape lifts at a corner after first use, add a drop of fabric glue under the fold and press flat for 24 hours before using the coaster again.

For routine care, shake the coasters outdoors to remove crumbs and surface debris. The looped pile traps dust: vacuum on a low suction setting with the attachment running across the pile, not against it. For a spilled drink, blot immediately with an absorbent cloth. Do not rub, as rubbing works the liquid deeper into the pile and can distort the loops. For more persistent stains, spot-clean with cool water and a small amount of wool wash, blotting and rinsing without soaking. Lay flat to dry. Do not machine-wash: the agitation distorts the pile and loosens the binding tape at the edges.
